panel.origin: The smallest and highest-pitched of the standard string family, and the most numerous instrument in the orchestra.
panel.chemistry: Bright, singing, and agile, with a huge expressive range from delicate whispers to soaring melody. The orchestra's main 'voice.'
panel.mixology: Held under the chin; strings are bowed or plucked (pizzicato), with the left hand stopping notes on the fingerboard.
panel.examples: The soaring main melody in almost any film score; the dazzling solo in Vivaldi's 'Four Seasons.'

panel.origin: The alto of the string family — slightly larger than the violin, with a deeper, warmer voice.
panel.chemistry: Mellow, dark, and slightly nasal, less brilliant than the violin but rich in the middle register. Often the 'inner glue' of string harmony.
panel.mixology: Played like a violin under the chin but larger; it reads its own clef (the alto clef) that few other instruments use.
panel.examples: The warm inner harmonies in Romantic symphonies; the lyrical solo in Berlioz's 'Harold in Italy.'

panel.origin: The tenor-bass of the strings, with one of the richest, most human-sounding voices in all of music.
panel.chemistry: Deep, warm, and resonant, capable of both a singing tenor melody and a powerful bass foundation. Often called the closest sound to the human voice.
panel.mixology: Played seated, held between the knees with a spike (endpin) resting on the floor, bowed or plucked.
panel.examples: The aching solo of Elgar's Cello Concerto; the rich bass line under any string section.

panel.origin: The largest and lowest string instrument, anchoring the entire orchestra's harmony from the bottom.
panel.chemistry: Deep, dark, and rumbling, providing the foundation that everything else is built on. Felt as much as heard.
panel.mixology: So large the player stands or perches on a tall stool; bowed or plucked, and a staple of jazz too.
panel.examples: The ominous low pulse opening many film scores; the walking bass line in jazz.

panel.origin: A large, triangular frame of 47 strings plucked by hand — the orchestra's most visually striking instrument.
panel.chemistry: Shimmering, ethereal, and cascading, capable of rippling glissandos that sweep across all the strings at once.
panel.mixology: Plucked with the fingers of both hands; seven foot pedals change the pitch of the strings to play in any key.
panel.examples: The magical, sparkling glissando that signals a dream or flashback; Debussy and Ravel's impressionist colors.

panel.origin: A silver tube blown across an open hole — the highest common woodwind, and one of the oldest instrument types on Earth.
panel.chemistry: Bright, pure, and airy, agile and birdlike in its upper range, breathy and gentle lower down.
panel.mixology: Held sideways; the player blows across the mouth hole (no reed) while fingers work the keys.
panel.examples: The bird calls and pastoral solos in Debussy's 'Prélude'; sparkling runs throughout the orchestra.

panel.origin: A tiny half-size flute that plays an octave higher — the highest-pitched instrument in the orchestra.
panel.chemistry: Brilliant, piercing, and shrill, able to cut clean through the loudest full-orchestra climax.
panel.mixology: Played exactly like the flute but much smaller; usually doubled by a flute player switching instruments.
panel.examples: The whistling, soaring line on top of marches like Sousa's 'Stars and Stripes Forever.'

panel.origin: A double-reed woodwind with a distinctive, slightly nasal, plaintive voice.
panel.chemistry: Reedy, penetrating, and expressive, with a poignant, almost vocal quality that carries easily over the orchestra.
panel.mixology: Sound is made by blowing through two reeds tied together that vibrate against each other.
panel.examples: The orchestra tunes to its steady 'A'; the haunting solo in the slow movement of many symphonies.

panel.origin: A single-reed woodwind with the widest range of any wind instrument and a remarkably smooth, flexible voice.
panel.chemistry: Warm and woody in its low range, bright and clear up high, capable of both jazz wail and orchestral elegance.
panel.mixology: A single reed vibrates against the mouthpiece; fingers and keys cover the holes along the body.
panel.examples: The famous swooping glissando opening Gershwin's 'Rhapsody in Blue'; the heart of any jazz band.

panel.origin: The bass of the woodwind family — a tall double-reed instrument with a comic and a melancholy side.
panel.chemistry: Deep, reedy, and characterful; gruff and almost humorous low down, but plaintive and lyrical in its tenor range.
panel.mixology: A long double tube folded back on itself; a curved metal tube (bocal) holds the double reed.
panel.examples: The lumbering grandfather theme in 'Peter and the Wolf'; the strange, high opening of Stravinsky's 'Rite of Spring.'

panel.origin: The English horn — a larger, lower cousin of the oboe with a deeply soulful tone (and despite the name, it's neither English nor a horn).
panel.chemistry: Mellow, melancholy, and warm, even more plaintive than the oboe, with a distinctive pear-shaped bell.
panel.mixology: Played like the oboe with a double reed, but longer, with a bent metal crook holding the reed.
panel.examples: The heartbreaking 'Going Home' melody in Dvořák's 'New World' Symphony slow movement.

panel.origin: The highest and most brilliant of the brass family, used for fanfares, melody, and sheer power.
panel.chemistry: Bright, bold, and heroic, able to ring out triumphantly or, when muted, sound intimate and jazzy.
panel.mixology: The player buzzes their lips into a cup mouthpiece; three valves change the length of tubing to change pitch.
panel.examples: Triumphant fanfares; the muted, smoky lead in a jazz combo; Aaron Copland's 'Fanfare for the Common Man.'

panel.origin: A coiled brass instrument with a mellow, noble voice that bridges the brass and woodwind sections.
panel.chemistry: Warm, round, and majestic, capable of soft heroic melody or powerful, ringing climaxes. Notoriously difficult to play cleanly.
panel.mixology: The player buzzes into a funnel mouthpiece, works rotary valves with the left hand, and shapes the tone with a hand in the bell.
panel.examples: The sweeping, heroic main themes of John Williams scores; the noble calls in Brahms and Strauss.

panel.origin: A brass instrument that changes pitch with a sliding tube instead of valves — the only one to do so.
panel.chemistry: Powerful, rich, and bold, capable of majestic chorale harmony or a comic, blaring slide.
panel.mixology: The player buzzes into the mouthpiece and moves a slide in and out to seven positions to change the pitch.
panel.examples: The grand chorale brass of Romantic symphonies; the smear and growl in big-band jazz.

panel.origin: The largest and lowest brass instrument, providing the foundation of the entire brass section.
panel.chemistry: Deep, round, and powerful, surprisingly agile and even gentle for its enormous size.
panel.mixology: The player buzzes into a large mouthpiece; valves control its many meters of coiled tubing. Played seated, resting on the lap.
panel.examples: The lumbering bass under the brass section; the famous solo portraying an elephant in 'Carnival of the Animals.'

panel.origin: Large tunable kettledrums — the heartbeat of the orchestra and the most important pitched percussion.
panel.chemistry: Deep, resonant, and dramatic, from a soft ominous rumble to a thunderous climax. Unlike most drums, they play definite pitches.
panel.mixology: Struck with felt-covered mallets; a foot pedal changes the tension of the drumhead to retune each drum.
panel.examples: The thunderous rolls and dramatic punctuation in Beethoven and Strauss; building tension in any film score.

panel.origin: A bright, sharp drum with wire snares stretched across the bottom head for its characteristic crisp rattle.
panel.chemistry: Crisp, cutting, and rhythmic, with a distinctive buzzing snap that drives a march or builds tension with a long roll.
panel.mixology: Struck with two wooden sticks; the snares vibrate against the lower head to create the buzzing tone.
panel.examples: The driving rattle of any military march; the relentless crescendo roll in Ravel's 'Boléro.'

panel.origin: The largest drum in the orchestra, producing a deep, booming, pitchless thud.
panel.chemistry: Deep, booming, and powerful — felt in the chest, used for dramatic impact and thunder.
panel.mixology: Struck with a large soft beater on a drum mounted upright; rolls use two beaters for a sustained rumble.
panel.examples: The cannon-like impacts in the '1812 Overture'; the thunder and dramatic accents across the orchestra.

panel.origin: Two large metal plates crashed together, or a single suspended cymbal struck with sticks.
panel.chemistry: Brilliant, crashing, and shimmering, from a delicate shimmer to an explosive climax.
panel.mixology: Crash cymbals are clashed together by hand; a suspended cymbal is rolled or struck with mallets or sticks.
panel.examples: The shining crash at a symphony's climax; the shimmering roll building to a peak.

panel.origin: A set of tuned wooden bars struck with mallets, producing a bright, woody, percussive melody.
panel.chemistry: Bright, hard, and bell-like with a short, dry 'click' — distinctly woody compared to its metal cousins.
panel.mixology: Wooden bars laid out like a piano keyboard are struck with hard mallets held in each hand.
panel.examples: The clattering, skeletal 'dance of the bones' in Saint-Saëns' 'Danse Macabre.'

panel.origin: A simple steel bar bent into a triangle — the smallest orchestral instrument with a surprisingly magical sound.
panel.chemistry: Bright, silvery, and ringing, a tiny shimmer of high sound that sparkles above the whole orchestra.
panel.mixology: Struck with a small metal beater; the player can mute it by hand or let it ring, and trill between two sides.
panel.examples: The fairy-tale sparkle in Tchaikovsky's 'Nutcracker'; the famous solo in Liszt's First Piano Concerto.

panel.origin: A small keyboard that strikes metal plates, producing a sweet, bell-like, music-box tone.
panel.chemistry: Delicate, sparkling, and ethereal — like tiny chiming bells, gentle and otherworldly.
panel.mixology: Looks like a small upright piano; pressing the keys makes hammers strike metal bars instead of strings.
panel.examples: The shimmering, magical 'Dance of the Sugar Plum Fairy' in Tchaikovsky's 'Nutcracker'; the Harry Potter theme.

panel.origin: A keyboard instrument that strikes strings with hammers — equally a solo star and an orchestral color.
panel.chemistry: Hugely versatile, from thunderous power to delicate intimacy, covering the full range of the orchestra by itself.
panel.mixology: Pressing a key throws a felt hammer at the strings; pedals sustain or soften the sound. 88 keys span over seven octaves.
panel.examples: Every piano concerto; the percussive, driving role in Stravinsky and modern orchestral works.

panel.origin: A keyboard that plucks its strings rather than striking them — the keyboard of the Baroque era before the piano.
panel.chemistry: Bright, crisp, and jangly, with a distinctive metallic 'twang.' It can't play loud or soft by touch — every note is the same volume.
panel.mixology: Pressing a key makes a small quill (plectrum) pluck the string. It has no dynamic control from finger pressure.
panel.examples: The continuo backbone of Bach and Vivaldi; the harpsichord sparkle throughout Baroque music.

panel.origin: A vast keyboard instrument that produces sound by pushing air through ranks of pipes — the largest instrument of all.
panel.chemistry: Enormous, powerful, and majestic, from a whisper to a wall of sound that can fill a cathedral and shake the floor.
panel.mixology: Played with multiple keyboards (manuals) and a pedalboard for the feet; 'stops' select which sets of pipes sound.
panel.examples: The thunderous opening of Bach's 'Toccata and Fugue'; the awe-inspiring climax of Saint-Saëns' 'Organ Symphony.'

panel.origin: The leader who unifies the whole orchestra, shaping tempo, dynamics, and interpretation — without making a sound.
panel.chemistry: Sets the pulse, balance, and emotional shape of the music, drawing a single coherent performance from a hundred players.
panel.mixology: Stands on a podium facing the orchestra, beating time with a baton in the right hand and shaping expression with the left.
panel.examples: The visible heartbeat of any live symphony; legendary figures like Bernstein, Karajan, and Dudamel.

panel.origin: The principal first violinist — the orchestra's second-in-command after the conductor.
panel.chemistry: Leads the string section, makes bowing decisions, and plays any violin solos. The link between players and conductor.
panel.mixology: Enters last before the conductor, signals the oboe to sound the tuning 'A,' and leads by example from the first chair.
panel.examples: Walks out to applause just before the conductor; plays the violin solos within orchestral works.

panel.origin: The 'principal' player who leads each section and plays its solos.
panel.chemistry: Each section (oboes, cellos, trumpets, etc.) has a principal who leads its phrasing and takes the solo passages.
panel.mixology: Sits at the front of the section, closest to the conductor, guiding entrances and the section's unified sound.
panel.examples: The clarinet, oboe, or horn player who stands to take a bow after a notable solo.

panel.origin: The largest body of the orchestra — violins, violas, cellos, and basses — forming its rich harmonic core.
panel.chemistry: The unified, blended sound of dozens of bowed strings; the warm foundation almost everything else sits on.
panel.mixology: Divided into first and second violins, violas, cellos, and basses, often playing in lush, layered harmony.
panel.examples: The wall of warm sound in any film score's emotional peak; the core of the Classical and Romantic orchestra.

panel.origin: The arrangement of players in a fan shape around the conductor, designed for balance and blend.
panel.chemistry: Strings sit closest in front, woodwinds in the middle, brass and percussion at the back — louder instruments further away.
panel.mixology: A traditional layout: first violins front-left, then around to cellos and basses on the right, winds behind, percussion at the rear.
panel.examples: The familiar curved arrangement you see at any symphony concert.

STRINGS.en: The strings make up more than half the players in a typical orchestra — a full symphony can have over 30 violins alone, which is why they so often carry the main melody.
STRINGS.es: The strings make up more than half the players in a typical orchestra — a full symphony can have over 30 violins alone, which is why they so often carry the main melody.
STRINGS.fr: The strings make up more than half the players in a typical orchestra — a full symphony can have over 30 violins alone, which is why they so often carry the main melody.
STRINGS.it: The strings make up more than half the players in a typical orchestra — a full symphony can have over 30 violins alone, which is why they so often carry the main melody.

WOODWINDS.en: The orchestra tunes to the oboe's 'A' for a practical reason: its piercing double-reed tone is steady and easy to hear, and being a fixed-pitch instrument it's hard to adjust on the fly anyway.
WOODWINDS.es: The orchestra tunes to the oboe's 'A' for a practical reason: its piercing double-reed tone is steady and easy to hear, and being a fixed-pitch instrument it's hard to adjust on the fly anyway.
WOODWINDS.fr: The orchestra tunes to the oboe's 'A' for a practical reason: its piercing double-reed tone is steady and easy to hear, and being a fixed-pitch instrument it's hard to adjust on the fly anyway.
WOODWINDS.it: The orchestra tunes to the oboe's 'A' for a practical reason: its piercing double-reed tone is steady and easy to hear, and being a fixed-pitch instrument it's hard to adjust on the fly anyway.

BRASS.en: A French horn unwound would stretch about 4 meters, a trumpet around 1.5 — all that tubing is coiled up, and the player changes notes partly just by tightening their lips.
BRASS.es: A French horn unwound would stretch about 4 meters, a trumpet around 1.5 — all that tubing is coiled up, and the player changes notes partly just by tightening their lips.
BRASS.fr: A French horn unwound would stretch about 4 meters, a trumpet around 1.5 — all that tubing is coiled up, and the player changes notes partly just by tightening their lips.
BRASS.it: A French horn unwound would stretch about 4 meters, a trumpet around 1.5 — all that tubing is coiled up, and the player changes notes partly just by tightening their lips.

PERCUSSION.en: Percussionists are the orchestra's specialists of one note at the perfect moment — a single cymbal crash or triangle ding, placed exactly right, can be the most memorable sound in the whole piece.
PERCUSSION.es: Percussionists are the orchestra's specialists of one note at the perfect moment — a single cymbal crash or triangle ding, placed exactly right, can be the most memorable sound in the whole piece.
PERCUSSION.fr: Percussionists are the orchestra's specialists of one note at the perfect moment — a single cymbal crash or triangle ding, placed exactly right, can be the most memorable sound in the whole piece.
PERCUSSION.it: Percussionists are the orchestra's specialists of one note at the perfect moment — a single cymbal crash or triangle ding, placed exactly right, can be the most memorable sound in the whole piece.

KEYBOARD & HARP.en: A concert grand piano holds about 230 strings under roughly 20 tons of combined tension — enough force that the frame has to be made of cast iron to keep from tearing itself apart.
KEYBOARD & HARP.es: A concert grand piano holds about 230 strings under roughly 20 tons of combined tension — enough force that the frame has to be made of cast iron to keep from tearing itself apart.
KEYBOARD & HARP.fr: A concert grand piano holds about 230 strings under roughly 20 tons of combined tension — enough force that the frame has to be made of cast iron to keep from tearing itself apart.
KEYBOARD & HARP.it: A concert grand piano holds about 230 strings under roughly 20 tons of combined tension — enough force that the frame has to be made of cast iron to keep from tearing itself apart.

ROLES & SECTIONS.en: The conductor never makes a sound, yet shapes everything — and the tradition of conducting with a silent baton only became standard in the 1800s; before that, leaders often kept time by loudly tapping a staff.
ROLES & SECTIONS.es: The conductor never makes a sound, yet shapes everything — and the tradition of conducting with a silent baton only became standard in the 1800s; before that, leaders often kept time by loudly tapping a staff.
ROLES & SECTIONS.fr: The conductor never makes a sound, yet shapes everything — and the tradition of conducting with a silent baton only became standard in the 1800s; before that, leaders often kept time by loudly tapping a staff.
ROLES & SECTIONS.it: The conductor never makes a sound, yet shapes everything — and the tradition of conducting with a silent baton only became standard in the 1800s; before that, leaders often kept time by loudly tapping a staff.
